title: When booking a priest isn't simple
subtitle: A booking primitive for ChurchDesk's platform, shipped first as funeral booking. Three stakeholder groups with opposite needs; one substrate the whole product could reuse.

hero_image.alt: The funeral home's booking screen — a full week of time slots for the Main Cemetery, with only the slots that resolve true against the priest's on-call rota, the cemetery's opening hours, and any standing commitments

sections.headline: When booking a priest isn't simple
sections.subtext: Booking a priest for a funeral looks like picking a date. Underneath sit rotating on-call rotas, cemetery opening hours, and a grieving family waiting for an answer. ChurchDesk asked me to lead product and design on a system that would keep the surface simple — and double as a platform booking primitive the rest of the product could reuse.

sections.headline: The problem was the phone
sections.content:
Before this system, booking a priest meant a chain of phone calls. A funeral home rang the parish office. The parish office checked who was on call, then rang the priest. The priest checked the cemetery's hours and their own calendar, then rang back. Every round of calls happened while a family waited.

The scheduling underneath is genuinely hard. Priests rotate on call, but many keep standing commitments to particular cemeteries. They plan availability about six weeks ahead. Each cemetery has its own opening hours. The first customer — the Evangelische Kirche von Kurhessen-Waldeck — ran the pilot in their Kurhessische Riviera cooperation region, where one priest's on-call rota touches several cemeteries at once.

What the customer brought to us was rare and useful: they had already run their own design-thinking workshop and arrived with a vision sheet that named the three stakeholder groups and the goals for each. I didn't run primary research from scratch — I started from their synthesis and worked from there.

Layered on top was a ChurchDesk business goal that the customer didn't ask for and didn't need to: build this so the platform can reuse it. Funeral booking would be the launch use case; the substrate had to be general enough to carry room rentals, equipment, counseling appointments — anything resource-and-availability-shaped.

The constraints were tight: a €20,000 budget, two months to development release.

images.alt: A flipchart sheet from a design-thinking workshop with the church customer — handwritten goals per stakeholder: funeral directors, parish assistants, priests
images.caption: The vision sheet from the church's own design-thinking session. The headline ‘Das Herumtelefonieren hat ein Ende’ — ‘no more phoning around’ — is the customer's phrase, not mine. So is the goal in red, ‘Damit Angehörige planen können’: so that bereaved families can plan.

sections.context: The customer's two-word brief, from the same workshop sheet: binding and uncomplicated. The whole design lives between those two words.

sections.headline: Three groups, three different needs
sections.content:
One system had to serve three groups, and they did not want the same thing.

**Funeral homes** (Bestatter) book several funerals a week. They needed to see what was free and get a confirmed answer quickly.

**Parish assistants** (Assistenzen) sat in the middle. They answered the funeral home, knew who was on call, and covered for vacancies. They needed a clear view of the whole rota.

**Priests** (Pfarrpersonen) needed predictability and a say over their own schedule. The system had to ask them, not assign them.

sections.quote: For a funeral home, it should feel like booking a priest: fast and reliable. For a priest, it should never feel like being booked. They needed to stay in control of their own time.
sections.context: The tension the whole system had to resolve

sections.headline: How I approached it
sections.content: I owned this initiative end-to-end as both product manager and designer. The dual role mattered: scoping the work as a platform primitive (not a funeral-only feature) is a product decision; keeping three stakeholder groups happy on one surface is a design decision. Running both meant the two decisions could reinforce each other instead of pulling against each other.

steps.label: Reframed the brief as a platform primitive
steps.description: The launch customer needed funeral booking. The business needed something that could pay back across the platform. I scoped the initiative as 'a generic booking primitive that ships first as funeral booking' — not as a funeral-only product. That framing decided which abstractions to build and which to scope out, and it set the bar for what 'done' looked like.

steps.label: Started from the customer's research, not mine
steps.description: The launch customer arrived with a vision sheet from their own design-thinking workshop — the three stakeholder groups and the goals per group already articulated in their own words. I started from that synthesis. With the parish team, I mapped the existing phone-based process and translated it into a workflow the platform could support. No primary user research by me; the customer had done the harder part.

steps.label: Designed for control, not just speed
steps.description: Funeral homes get speed. Priests get control. I showed funeral homes only the times that were genuinely free. And I framed every booking as a request the priest confirms, so no one is scheduled without agreeing first.

steps.label: Built on what ChurchDesk already had
steps.description: ChurchDesk already had a calendar with people and resources attached to events, and a form builder used everywhere else in the product. I extended both instead of building a separate booking surface. That decision is how the work fit the budget and the timeline — and it's why priests didn't have to learn a new tool, and it's also what made the substrate genuinely reusable for non-funeral booking scenarios later.

steps.label: Prototyped with engineering
steps.description: I prototyped and iterated directly with the engineering team. The screens were designed as React Native components on the ShadCn system, with component specs and annotated flows for handoff.

sections.headline: Why it stays simple when the underneath is complex
sections.content:
Three design decisions did the work of keeping the surface clean. None of them are visible to a funeral home — they're the reason the funeral home only ever sees green time slots.

**On-call lives in the priest's calendar, not in a separate booking-page admin.** This is the design choice that most distinguishes the system from Calendly-style booking tools. In Calendly, you set your availability inside a separate booking-page settings area. Here, a priest sets their on-call rotation as recurring events in the same ChurchDesk calendar where they already plan their week. The calendar is where priests live; we extended that surface instead of asking them to learn a new one. Recurring events handle the rota; vacation cover slots into the same view.

**A priest sets availability for several cemeteries in one action.** In the pilot region, one priest's on-call shift covers multiple cemeteries at once. Setting each one separately would be six entries a week of friction. The availability model lets a single recurring event apply across the relevant cemeteries.

**The booking form is a custom form, not a fixed one.** ChurchDesk's existing form builder is reused inside the booking flow. Funeral homes don't get a generic booking page with name/email/date fields — they answer the questions the parish actually needs (deceased details, funeral type, cemetery section, sterbeurkunde delivery). Calendly cannot do this; its booking form is fixed and the form-builder is a separate product. Reusing the form builder meant churches with idiosyncratic intake questions could ship without engineering work.

images.alt: The priest's ChurchDesk calendar with a Verfügbarkeit (availability) modal open — type ‘Bereitschaft Beerdigung’ (on-call for funerals), user Pfarrer Meissner, multiple cemeteries selected at once (Hauptfriedhof, Waldfriedhof) with more available in the dropdown
images.caption: The decision that distinguishes the system from Calendly, captured in one screen. The priest sets on-call rotation as a recurring event in the same calendar where they already plan their week — and a single event covers several cemeteries at once.

images.alt: The funeral home's booking-form step — date and time slot at the top, a Select staff member radio list, and a custom form with funeral-domain fields the parish configured itself: Name des/der Verstorbenen, Art der Bestattung (Erdbestattung), Ort der Trauerfeier, Angehörigen-Kontakt
images.caption: The form is configured by the parish using ChurchDesk's existing form builder. Note the fields: name of the deceased, type of burial, place of the memorial service, family contact. Calendly cannot have these fields; its booking form is fixed. We just reused infrastructure that was already in the product.

sections.headline: How the system fits together
sections.content:
The design rests on one composition: a priest sets their availability in the calendar, the cemetery's opening hours and any standing commitments are layered on, and what remains is what the funeral home sees. A slot only reaches the funeral home if every constraint resolves true.

Every booking is a request, not an automatic booking. The priest confirms or declines. That single decision is what kept the system from feeling like priests were being booked over their heads.

The availability model was deliberately general. The business goal was never a funeral-booking feature — it was a booking primitive that could carry funeral booking as its launch use case and then expand across the platform: room rentals, equipment reservations, counseling appointments, anything resource-and-availability-shaped. Funeral booking shipped first; the substrate was the real deliverable.

sections.headline: What changed

metrics.label: Coordination time
metrics.before: Days of phone calls between three parties
metrics.after: Hours to arrange a funeral booking — around 70% less time

metrics.label: Booking acceptance rate
metrics.value: 90%, because funeral homes only ever saw times that were genuinely free

metrics.label: Adoption
metrics.value: Became a core ChurchDesk feature, used beyond the first region, across 20+ cemeteries

sections.narrative: Coordination dropped from days to hours. The 90% acceptance rate came directly from the filtering: if a funeral home could see a time, the priest could take it. The longer-term outcome — the one that justified the investment to the business — was the availability model itself: built once for funeral booking, reusable across the platform without redesign. The funeral feature was the visible deliverable; the platform primitive was the strategic one.

sections.headline: What I took from it
sections.content:
The best multi-stakeholder design does not try to make everyone happy with one interface. It gives each group the right level of control and visibility. Funeral homes got speed. Priests got control. Parish assistants got oversight.

The tension between those needs is the actual design work — and on this project a lot of that translation was possible because the customer had already done the synthesis themselves. They knew their community: bereaved families, busy funeral directors, stretched parish offices. They handed us the goals. My job was the translation, not the discovery.

The other lesson is about leverage — and it's where the product role mattered as much as the design role. The most consequential move on this project wasn't a design choice; it was a scoping choice. Building a funeral-booking feature would have taken roughly the same time as building a generic booking primitive that ships as funeral booking. The first delivers a feature. The second delivers a platform capability that pays back for years. Reusing existing platform infrastructure (the calendar, the form builder) was what made the second option viable inside the budget.

executive.summary: Owned product and design on ChurchDesk's booking primitive — a platform feature scoped to ship first as funeral booking and reuse across the product. Delivered in two months on a €20,000 budget; 70% less coordination time, 90% booking acceptance. Started from the launch customer's own design-thinking output rather than primary research; extended the existing calendar and form builder rather than building a new admin surface.
executive.angle: A platform primitive that launched as a feature

blog.summary: Funeral planning is hard enough without phone-tag between funeral homes, churches, and cemeteries. Here is how the booking system made it simple for everyone involved — and how the scoping choice (build a platform primitive, not a funeral feature) decided everything downstream.
blog.angle: Designing for stakeholders who want opposite things — and scoping for a platform, not a feature

meta.confidentiality_note: First customer (Evangelische Kirche von Kurhessen-Waldeck) is named with their consent — they are publicly identified in the ChurchDesk conference talk from 2025-02-26.
